cards.front: In Macbeth, what spiritual and psychological disturbances does Macbeth report immediately after killing Duncan?
cards.back: Macbeth cannot say “Amen” and hears a voice cry that he has murdered sleep.
cards.explanation: In the chamber aftermath, Macbeth reports hearing the sleepers’ prayers but being unable to answer “Amen.” His imagined voice then identifies him with the destruction of innocent sleep.
cards.memoryTip: Retrieve the disturbance in two stages: blocked blessing → accusing voice about sleep.

choices.text: Macbeth cannot say “Amen” and hears a voice cry that he has murdered sleep.
choices.feedback: Correct: the scene joins a blocked prayer to the imagined accusation about sleep.

choices.text: Macbeth says “Amen” easily and returns the daggers himself.
choices.feedback: This reverses his inability to pray and confuses his response to the daggers.

choices.text: Lady Macbeth hears the accusing voice while Macbeth sleeps peacefully.
choices.feedback: This transfers Macbeth’s reported experience to Lady Macbeth and reverses the loss of sleep.

choices.text: Macbeth hears a blessing promising him peaceful sleep.
choices.feedback: This reverses the voice’s accusation that Macbeth has murdered sleep.

cards.sourceExcerpt: I could not say “Amen,” When they did say, “God bless us.”
